segments.s295-g129: فرستاد is a simple past (3rd sing.) of فرستادن, here ‘he sent/dispatched’. The phrase بر هر سوی (= ‘toward every direction’) follows the verb as a directional adjunct, and لشکری (an indefinite noun functioning as the object) names what was sent: ‘troops/an army.’
segments.s295-g130: بسی is an adverb meaning ‘many/much’ (here ‘many’), followed by the plural noun چاره‌ها ‘remedies/expedients’. ساخت is the past tense ‘made/devised’ that takes چاره‌ها as its object. از هر دری is a prepositional phrase ‘from every quarter/door/kind’ qualifying the source or manner of those measures.
segments.s295-g131: چنین بود is a verbless/existential phrase rendered as ‘thus was’ (بود here as past ‘was’). فرمان یزدان پاک is a noun phrase in apposition: ‘the decree/command of the pure God’ (یزدان = God, پاک = pure, postmodifying یزدان).
segments.s295-g132: که introduces a content clause (‘that …’). بیدادگر modifies شاه as an epithet ‘the unjust/tyrannical (one)’, while گردد is the present/subjunctive-like form of گشتن meaning ‘become/come to be’ and here expresses the destined result: ‘should become’. هلاک is a predicative noun ‘ruin/destruction’, completing the clause: ‘that the tyrant king should be ruined.’
segments.s295-g133: شب تیره is a noun + adjective ('the dark/ominous night'). بنشست is past ‘sat/settled’ (here impersonal/poetic: ‘night fell’). با introduces the accompaniment ‘with’, and بخردان is the plural oblique of بخرد (‘the wise ones’), marking who accompanied that night.
segments.s295-g134: This phrase lists those accompanying the night: جهاندیده (‘worldly‑wise’ or experienced) and رای زن موبدان. رای زن is a compound where رای (opinion/counsel) combined with زن (‘one who strikes/puts forth’ as an agentive element) yields ‘counsel‑giving’, and موبدان is the plural of موبد ‘mōbed/priests’. The whole reads ‘the worldly‑wise and the counsel‑giving mobeds.’
segments.s295-g135: ز is the poetic form of از ‘from’ used here as a preposition ‘in/with respect to’. هرگونه ‘in every way’ is an adverbial phrase, با او ‘with him’ indicates the addressee, سخن ساختند is a verbal expression (‘they made speech / spoke to him’): ساختند is past plural ‘they made/composed’, so together ‘they spoke with him in every possible way.’
segments.s295-g136: جهان را uses the object marker را to topicalize ‘the world’. چپ و راست is an idiomatic paired adverbial ‘left and right’ (meaning ‘all around’ or ‘helter‑skelter’). انداختند is past plural ‘they threw/caused to be cast’, so the clause says they ‘threw/upset the world right and left’ (i.e., caused disorder everywhere).
segments.s295-g57: همان روز is a time expression ('that/same day') placed before the subject; کیخسرو is the subject; آنجا رسید is a past-tense clause with رسید (past of رسیدن) meaning 'arrived/reached'. Word order (time — subject — verb) is common in epic Persian.
segments.s295-g58: This is a compact locative/result clause: لشکر کشید is a past transitive action ('drew/arrayed/stretched his army') with لشکر as the object and کشید the past of کشیدن. زمین ... تا کوه marks the spatial extent — 'across the land, from mountain to mountain' — with تا indicating the span. Note the terse, poetic omission of additional prepositions or ezāfe links.
segments.s295-g59: به زنهار شد is an idiomatic predication meaning 'was put on warning / went into alarm' (به + noun + شدن). لشکر ما همه is the subject phrase ('our entire army'); note the possessive pronoun ما follows لشکر, and همه ('all/entire') emphasizes totality.
segments.s295-g60: هراسان شد is a simple past result clause ('became frightened'). از introduces the cause ('because of' / 'on account of') and is followed by the compound noun بی‌شبانی رمه — بی‌شبانی ('lack of night-rest / sleeplessness') qualifies رمه ('flock/herd'); classical Persian often juxtaposes such genitive relations without an explicit ezāfe.
segments.s295-g61: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (classical sequence: چو + past verb). شاه is the subject, این سخن the object/phrase 'this speech/news', and خیره گشت is a predication 'became stunned/astonished' with گشت (past of گشتن) as copula-like verb.
segments.s295-g62: This line has two coordinated result clauses. سیه گشت 'he grew dark/gloomy' (سیه used figuratively), then و introduces a following coordinated clause where چشم و دلش ('his eye and his heart' — the possessive clitic ش attaches to دل but applies to both coordinated nouns) تیره گشت 'became dark/bleak'. گشت is the past copular/phase verb in each clause.
segments.s295-g63: خروشان here is a manner adverb/adjectival participle ('roaringly' / 'with a roar') modifying the verb phrase فرود آمد ('came down/descended' — آمد in past). از تخت عاج is an ablative phrase 'from the ivory throne' (تخت عاج = 'ivory throne').
segments.s295-g64: به پیش بزرگان is a prepositional/dative locative ('before/in the presence of the great ones') where به + پیش expresses 'in front of / before'. بینداخت is the past form of انداختن here meaning 'threw/cast', and تاج is the direct object 'the crown' — together 'he cast the crown before the nobles.'
segments.s295-g1: سپهدار توران is a nominal phrase in the genitive: 'the commander (سپهدار) of Turan (توران)'. از آن سوی جاج is a prepositional phrase introduced by poetic از 'from', with آن سوی 'that side/direction' and جاج as a place-name; it specifies the commander’s origin/location and attaches to the subject.
segments.s295-g2: نشسته is an adjectival/predicative participle meaning 'seated' (used as the main verbal predicate here). به آرام is an adverbial phrase where به is used in the adverbial sense 'by/—ly' and آرام 'calm(ly)'. بر تخت عاج is a locative prepositional phrase 'upon an ivory throne' (تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory').
segments.s295-g3: دوباره is an adverb 'again' modifying the whole clause. ز (poetic form of از) introduces the source 'from the army' (لشکر), and هزاران هزار is a poetic pluralizing/repetition meaning 'thousands and thousands' (i.e., innumerable thousands); the whole phrase marks origin and magnitude.
segments.s295-g4: سپه بود is an existential/past statement: سپه 'host/army' + بود 'was' (past copula), here meaning 'there were hosts'. با آلت کارزار is an adjunct introduced by با 'with', where آلت 'instruments' takes the genitive/compound کارزار 'of battle' to mean 'implements/weapons of war'.
segments.s295-g5: نشسته is the predicate 'seated/sitting'; همه 'all' quantifies the following coordinated nouns. خلخ و سرکشان is a coordinate phrase; خلخ is best read here as 'anklets', while سرکشان is a poetic word whose exact sense is not supplied by the given senses list (so its precise meaning in context is uncertain). The whole phrase describes what/who was seated.
segments.s295-g6: همی is an archaic/poetic marker indicating ongoing or iterative aspect ('still/ever'); it scopes over the coordinated nouns سرفرازان و گردنکشان, which are plural nouns meaning 'the proud/victorious' and 'the proud/haughty ones' respectively. Grammatically this continues the description of those who are seated (links back to نشسته).
segments.s295-g7: به marks location/toward: 'to/at the border'. مرز کروشان زمین is a genitive chain 'the border of Kroshan's land' (کروشان as a place/person name, زمین 'land'). هرچ بود is an existential clause 'whatever there was' (هرچ 'whatever', بود past 'was').
segments.s295-g8: This is a coordinated list introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from': ز برگ درخت 'from the leaves of the tree(s)', ز کشت 'from the crops' and درود 'greetings/blessings' (here listed as items that existed or were offered). Each item is linked by و 'and'.
segments.s295-g161: پراگند is an adverb/adjective meaning 'scattered' used frontally to describe the state of the animals. هر سو is a frozen phrase 'every direction/side' (هر = each/every + سو = side/direction). هیونی is the plural/collective notion 'beasts/animals' (poetic form), and دوان is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'running/ran' (a participial/elliptical form used in narrative). The whole segment literally reads 'scattered, every direction animals running.'
segments.s295-g162: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'one' / 'a certain' (introducing a single individual). مرد is 'man' but in epic context it often implies a valiant person/warrior. هشیار is an adjective 'alert, watchful', and روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑soul(ed)' or 'clear‑minded / good‑spirited'. The noun phrase is descriptive and stands in contrast to the panicked animals.
segments.s295-g163: ببینید is the 2nd‑plural (or polite) imperative 'look/see' used in reported speech; poetic narration often preserves the imperative followed by گفت ('he said'). گفت here introduces the quoted command. از چپ و دست راست literally 'from the left and (from the) right hand/side' — دست راست = 'right hand/right side'. Word order places the quotation verb before or after the reported imperative for stylistic inversion.
segments.s295-g164: که here introduces a subordinate (indirect) question/explanatory clause: 'where ... is'. بالا is 'height/upper extent' or 'front/upward extent' and پهنای is 'width/breadth' (of the army). لشکر is the noun 'army/host'. کجا + ست is the inverted copular question 'where is ...' (کجاست = 'where is it'). So the clause asks/commands to look so as to see where the army's extent (height and width) is located.
segments.s295-g165: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بازآمد is a past/telic verb 'returned / came back'. از هر سوی = 'from every side' (از = from, هر = every, سوی = side/direction). رزمساز is a compound meaning 'war‑maker'—in context 'warrior/one who fought in the battle'. The segment sets the time and agent for the speech that follows.
segments.s295-g166: چنین 'thus/so' modifies گفت ('he said thus'), and با introduces the indirect object 'to' (با شاه = 'to the king' in older usage; با + person = speaking to that person). گردن‌فراز is an epithet meaning 'neck‑raising' i.e. 'proud, haughty' and modifies شاه. The phrase literally 'thus he spoke with the proud king' = 'he thus addressed the proud king.'
segments.s295-g167: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). چندین is an indefinite quantifier 'several / many a'. سپه is 'army/host' and را is the definite object marker marking سپه as the (logical) object of the need being discussed. بر این دشت جنگ is a genitive/locative expression 'for war on this plain' or 'to fight on this plain' (بر = on/for, این = this, دشت = plain, جنگ = war/battle). The phrase frames the context for what is needed.
segments.s295-g168: علف is 'grass / fodder' (necessity for animals), and باید here is impersonal 'is needed / it is necessary' (not a volitional 'must' of a person). و is the conjunction 'and'. ساز in this context means 'equipment / implements / means' (not only 'musical instrument' — epic usage often refers to gear or means). و جای درنگ = 'and a place to tarry/encamp' (جای = place, درنگ = pause / to stay). Together the clause lists necessities for maintaining many troops on the plain.
segments.s295-g65: خروشی ز لشکر بر آمد به درد is a compact, poetic clause: خروشی (a roar/clamor) is the subject, ز لشکر 'from the host' gives the source with the preposition ز, and بر آمد (the compound verb برآمد) is the past tense 'arose/rose up'. به درد here links the event to an emotional state — 'in/with pain' — so the whole reads 'a cry rose from the host in sorrow.' Note the b- prefix in برآمد that combines with آمد to form 'arise' and the inverted, compressed word order typical of poetry.
segments.s295-g66: رخ نامداران شد از درد زرد is a predicative clause: رخ 'face' is modified by نامداران 'of the renowned' (genitive relation), شد is the past of شدن 'became', and از درد زرد indicates cause and result: 'pale because of grief'. Word order places the patient/affected noun (رخ نامداران) before the copular verb شد, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s295-g67: ز بیگانه خیمه بپرداختند: ز بیگانه 'from/because of the stranger(s)' gives the source; خیمه is the direct object 'tent(s)', and بپرداختند is a past plural verb with the b- prefix. In older/poetic Persian بپرداختن with a tangible object often means to take down, remove, or deal with the tent(s); grammatically, the verb is plural (they) and the clause is compactly ordered (source — object — verb).
segments.s295-g68: ز خویشان یکی انجمن ساختند: ز here functions like 'from/among' to mark the group (the kinsmen), یکی 'one' (= one person / someone) is the subject in postnominal position, and انجمن ساختند is a past plural predicate 'they made/formed an assembly'. The compound انجمن ساختند uses ساختند (past plural of ساختن) as 'formed' or 'constituted'.
segments.s295-g69: از آن درد بگریست افراسیاب: از آن درد 'from that pain' gives the cause, بگریست is the simple past 'he wept' (the b- prefix + گریست), and the name افراسیاب follows the verb — a common poetic order where the verb precedes the subject. The clause states cause → action (he wept because of that pain).
segments.s295-g70: همی کند موی و همی ریخت آب uses the iterative particle همی to show continuous or repeated actions: همی کند موی literally 'he kept doing (کند) hair' — an idiom for tearing or pulling out hair — and همی ریخت آب 'he kept pouring water', where ریخت (here) carries the sense of 'pouring/flowing' (i.e. tears flowing). The verbs are present-stem forms used in a habitual/continuous sense by the poet.
segments.s295-g71: همی گفت زار این جهانبین من: همی گفت is the continuous past/iterative 'he kept saying'; زار 'wretched/alas' is an interjection-like lament, and این جهانبین من is a vocative/appositive phrase 'this world-seer of mine' (the noun followed by the possessive pronoun). Word order places the verb before the vocative phrase, a poetic arrangement.
segments.s295-g72: سوار سرافراز رویین من is a noun phrase in apposition describing the addressee: سوار 'rider/horseman' followed by two postpositive adjectives/specificators سرافراز 'proud/triumphant' and رویین 'iron-clad/armoured', with من as the poet's possessive 'my' placed after the noun phrase — a common poetic placement for possessives.
segments.s295-g217: این عبارت یک عبارت توضیحی است: که introduces a relative clause ('whose' / 'who'), سالارشان is a possessed noun ('their commander'), بود is the past copula ('was'), and پسر here is a predicative noun ('son'). Word order is typical Persian: subject/possessed (سالارشان) + copula (بود) + predicate noun (پنجم پسر).
segments.s295-g218: یکی نامور is an appositive phrase meaning 'a certain/famed one' (یکی = 'a certain', نامور = 'renowned'). گرد پرخاشخر functions as a proper name or epithet ('Gerd‑Parkhashkhar'); grammatically it follows that apposition and names often follow one another in older Persian. Treat گرد + پرخاشخر together as the personal epithet.
segments.s295-g219: ورا is the object pronoun 'to him' / 'him'; خواندندی here is an archaic/perhaps poetic verbal form meaning 'they called' (not the modern readable senses listed), and گو is a quotative/introductory particle 'so-called' / 'called'. گردگیر is the name or epithet applied (literally 'dust‑gatherer' or 'dust‑cloak'). The whole segment = 'they called him Gerdgir'.
segments.s295-g220: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that' (here giving the reason or description). بر is the locative/prepositional 'on/upon', کوه is 'mountain', بگذاشتی is an archaic past form 'you/one had placed/left' (here narratively 'had placed/left'), and تیغ و تیر = 'sword and arrow' (tiğ = sword, tir = arrow). Grammatically this clause explains why he bore that name.
segments.s295-g221: دمور و جرنجاش are two coordinated names or nominatives (proper names here), followed by با او ('with him') where با is the comitative preposition and او is the pronoun 'him'. برفت is an archaic past verb meaning 'went' / 'set off'. The sequence means 'Damur and Jeranjash went with him.'
segments.s295-g222: به یاری is a prepositional phrase 'to the aid of' or 'with the help of' (به + یاری). جهن is a proper name (here the person being helped), سرافراز is an epithet/adjective 'proud, lofty, triumphant' modifying the name that follows, and تفت is here a name or epithet attached to Jahan. Together: 'to aid Jahan, the lofty Taft.'
segments.s295-g223: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', گردان here means 'troops' or 'regiments', و links the following noun, and جنگ آوران is a compound meaning 'war‑bringers' i.e. 'warriors' or 'fighters'. Together this names the group ('from the regiments and the warriors').
segments.s295-g224: برفتند is the third‑person plural past 'they went/advanced/proceeded'. با is the comitative 'with', خنجر is 'dagger', and کارزار means 'battle/field of battle'. The segment states their action and instrument/place: 'they went with dagger to the battle.'
segments.s295-g25: کنون is an adverb meaning “now/presently” placed before the clause. The noun phrase نام کندز ("the name of Kunduz") is the theme, followed by the prepositional phrase به بیکند ("to/into Bikand"). The verb گشت is the past intransitive "became/turned (into)" — so literally "now the name of Kunduz became Bikand." This is a typical Persian word order: temporal adverb → topic → complement → verb.
segments.s295-g26: زمانه is the subject meaning "the age/era." پر + از forms the adjectival phrase "full of" (پر از ...). بند و ترفند are the coordinated nouns giving what the age is full of (here "snares/fetters and stratagems"). گشت is the past intransitive "became," so the clause reads "the age became full of bonds and stratagems."
segments.s295-g27: نبیره فریدون is a genitival noun phrase meaning "a scion/descendant of Fereydun." بد is an epithet placed before the proper name افراسیاب, giving "the evil/bad Afrasiab." This is appositional: two identifiers for the same person (scion of Fereydun — the bad Afrasiab).
segments.s295-g28: ز کندز به رفتن is a prepositional phrase with the infinitive رفتن: "to go from Kunduz." ز is the poetic variant of از "from." The verb نکردی is a past negative meaning "did not do/make" (here: "did not make haste"); شتاب is the noun "haste." Together they mean "did not hasten" (literally "did not make haste to go"). Note classical morphology: نکردی functions here as the past negative for the subject mentioned earlier.
segments.s295-g29: خود و ویژگانش is the coordinated subject "he himself and his distinguished ones (his nobles/retainers)." نشسته is a verbal adjective/past participle meaning "seated" or "sat down." به دشت is the locative phrase "in/onto the plain." The clause states their position: "he and his retinue sat in the plain."
segments.s295-g30: سپهر از سپاهش is a causal/agentive phrase: سپهر "the sky/heaven," از سپاهش literally "because of his army" (از marks cause). The clitic ش on سپاه is the third-person possessive "his." همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle often used before verbs; خیره گشت is "became astonished/stared" (خیره as adjective/participle + گشت "became"). Together: "the sky, on account of his host, was astonished."
segments.s295-g31: ز دیبای چینی is a material phrase "of Chinese brocade" using poetic ز (= از) to indicate material/source. سراپرده is the noun "inner curtain/inner chamber." بود is the past copula "was." Together they state existence/material: "there was an inner curtain of Chinese brocade."
segments.s295-g32: فراوان is an adverb/adjective "plentiful/abundant." به پرده درون is a locative phrase "into the inner curtain/within the curtain." برده بود is a compound of the past participle برده ("taken/brought") plus the auxiliary بود, forming a past perfect/passive sense: "had been brought/taken." So the clause means "many had been brought into the inner curtain."
segments.s295-g225: This is a noun phrase made of two adjectival epithets followed by the noun with a possessive suffix. جهاندیده (prepositive adjective) = "world‑wise/experienced." نستوه is a poetic epithet attached to the same head; here it functions as an adjective modifying سالار. سالارشان = "their commander/leader," where -شان is the 3rd‑person plural possessive suffix. Word order: Adj + Adj + N+poss.
segments.s295-g226: This is an appositive noun phrase: پشنگ (proper name) + دلاور (epithet, "brave/valiant") in apposition, followed by نگهدارشان = "their guardian/keeper" (نگهدار + -شان). The three parts form a coordinated description of leadership/defense: name → epithet → role with possessive suffix linking it to the same group as سالارشان above.
segments.s295-g227: همان here points back to previously mentioned men: "that same." The preposition از introduces the group taken from a larger set: "from the heroes/warriors (یلان)." ترکمان identifies their ethnicity. Word order: demonstrative + از + noun (plural) + ethnic identifier.
segments.s295-g228: برفتند is the simple past tense (3rd‑person plural) meaning "they went/advanced." با introduces accompanying instruments: گرز (mace/club), then coordination with و and تیر (arrow), و and کمان (bow). This is a straightforward verb + prepositional phrase listing weapons.
segments.s295-g229: سپهبد (spahbed) is the title/subject. چو is the simile preposition "like/as," introducing اغریرث (a poetic/name figure) in apposition, followed by جنگجوی "warrior" which further apposes or describes اغریرث. The whole phrase reads as a simile: "the spahbed, like Aghrirath the warrior."
segments.s295-g230: This is a relative clause introduced by که ("who/which") modifying سپهبد. با خون یکی داشتی آب جوی is a slightly inverted classical word order: آب جوی ("stream‑water") is the noun phrase, یکی داشتی functions as a predicate meaning "was one/united (with)" or "had become one," and با خون (= "with blood") marks what it was united with. داشتی is a past form of داشتن (to have/become) here, so the clause means "whose stream ran as one with blood" (i.e., the stream was mingled with blood).
segments.s295-g231: وز here is an archaic fused preposition/conjunction (effectively "and from" / "from") introducing the source or group. آن نامور تیغ‌زن is a noun phrase "that renowned swordsman(s)/swordsmen;" سی هزار is the numeral phrase "thirty thousand" which is the quantity taken from that group. Classical Persian often places numerals before/after the counted noun; here the whole phrase signals partitive selection.
segments.s295-g232: گزین کرد is the compound past tense "chose/selected" (گزین = to choose + کرد = did/made). Shah (شاه) is the subject placed after the verb (Per­sian permits subject‑after‑verb order). از marks source/partitive and در کارزار is a locative/idiomatic phrase: در here is used as "gate/portal" or figuratively "the field of battle," so از در کارزار = "for/from the battle (line)." کارزار = "battle."
segments.s295-g49: همه خستگان is a nominal phrase 'all the weary' (همه + plural noun). از پس یکدگر is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'one after another' or 'in succession' (از + پس + یکدگر), placed after the noun to indicate order.
segments.s295-g50: رسیدند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they arrived/came'. گریان is a predicative adjective/participle 'weeping' describing manner; و links adjectives; خسته جگر is a poetic collocation where خسته ('weary') qualifies جگر ('heart, seat of feeling'), meaning 'with hearts grown weary'.
segments.s295-g51: همی is an adverb indicating continuous or repeated action ('kept on'). هر کسی is 'each person'. یاد کرد is the light verbal phrase 'made remembrance' = 'recalled/mentioned' (یاد as noun combined with کرد). آنچ دید is a relative nominal clause 'that which he saw' (آنچ = 'that which', followed by verb دید).
segments.s295-g52: وزان links the clause to what precedes ('and from that'). بد is a noun here 'evil/misfortune'. کز introduces a relative clause 'which/from which'; ایران is the source 'Iran'; بدیشان رسید is a past construction 'reached them' or 'befell them' (رسید used impersonally/causatively).
segments.s295-g53: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing sources. پیران is the plural 'elders'. و simply coordinates items. لهاک appears here as a listed name or term; given the context it is functioning as a coordinated item (proper-name usage). فرشیدورد is a personal name; the sequence is a simple list of sources.
segments.s295-g54: وزان again ties back 'and from that / and from them'. نامداران is a nominative plural 'the famed/renowned ones'. روز نبرد is a genitive-like noun phrase 'the day of battle' identifying which famed men are meant—i.e. famed men of the battle day.
segments.s295-g55: کز introduces a relative/exclamatory clause 'what/that which'. ایشان is the object 'them/their'. چه آمد is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what came/happened'. به روی سپاه is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of the host/army' (به + روی + سپاه), where روی is used poetically as 'face/at the front of'.
segments.s295-g56: چه here is exclamatory 'what (a)'. زاری is 'lamentation/cry of sorrow'. رسید is past 'reached/was heard' (impersonal use). اندر آن رزمگاه is the locative 'in that battlefield' (اندر = in, آن = that, رزمگاه = battlefield).
segments.s295-g73: This is a nominal phrase: an epithet plus two names. جهانجوی is an epithet placed before the proper name (literally “world‑seeking”), و is the simple conjunction joining the two names, and فرشیدورد is a personal name. In classical Persian poetry the epithet precedes the name without an ezāfe marker.
segments.s295-g74: A coordinated noun phrase describing the force: سواران “horsemen” and گردان “troop.” روز نبرد is a genitival/locative grouping (literally “day of battle”); in poetry the ezāfe or preposition linking these nouns is often omitted, so گردان روز نبرد reads as “the troop (on/of) the battle‑day.”
segments.s295-g75: The phrase begins with the prepositional source از این جنگ “from this battle.” پور و برادر نماند is a coordinated object followed by the verb نماند. نماند here is the past negative existential sense “was not left / did not remain,” applying to the coordinated nouns (i.e., no son or brother remained).
segments.s295-g76: Another coordinated nominal series (بزرگان و سالار و لشکر) followed by the same verb نماند; the verb governs all three coordinated nouns and again expresses that none of these were left. In poetry the list is presented without additional particles or ezāfe.
segments.s295-g77: بنالید is a past tense (classical form often lacking the final -ند) meaning “they wailed/lamented.” The conjunction و links the clause to the next. بر زد is a compound of the prepositional adverb بر (“upon/on”) plus the past verb زد (“struck”); یکی باد سرد follows as the affected/energetic subject/object in inverted poetic order: “a certain cold wind struck (upon them).”
segments.s295-g78: پس آنگه (“then, thereafter”) marks the sequence. یکی is the indefinite “one” (= ‘one of them’ or ‘a certain one’). سخت is an adverbial use meaning “firmly/strongly,” سوگند is “oath,” and خورد forms the common classical compound سوگند خورد meaning “to swear / to take an oath.” خورد here is an auxiliary in a fixed expression (archaic usage).
segments.s295-g79: به یزدان که is an oath formula “by God, that …” where به… invokes God as the oath‑bearer. که introduces the content clause. بیزارم is first‑person present “I am weary/averse,” governed by از تخت و گاه (“of/from the throne and the court/place”); گاه here is a poetic/archaic word for place/seat and is coordinated with تخت by و.
segments.s295-g80: A conditional clause introduced by اگر (“if”). نیز is “also”; بیند (classical third‑person present/subjunctive of دیدن) functions as the verb “sees” or “should see” in the conditional. سر من کلاه is a verbless predication in which سر من (“my head”) is equated with کلاه (“a hat”)—the copula or comparative sense is omitted, typical of terse poetic phrasing.
segments.s295-g177: This nominal phrase describes the subject: 'سپهدار' (a military title) followed by the emphatic/reflexive 'خود' ('himself'). 'دیده' is a past participle ('having seen') modifying what follows, and 'بد روزگار' is a genitive-like collocation meaning 'ill‑fate' or 'unfavourable times' that the commander has seen. Word order (title + self + participle + noun phrase) is typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s295-g178: The verb phrase 'نرفتی به گفتار آموزگار' expresses negative past (did not go/obey). 'نرفتی' here functions as a past negative ('did not go' / 'did not follow') with the object introduced by 'به' + 'گفتار' ('to the speech/words'). 'آموزگار' names whose words they are: 'the teacher/adviser'. In context this means 'did not follow the teacher's advice.'
segments.s295-g179: 'بیاراست' is a past/transitive verb meaning 'he arrayed/arranged/adorned' and is placed before its objects (a common poetic order). 'قلب و جناح سپاه' names the objects: 'قلب' here means the army's core/centre and 'جناح' its wing/flank; 'سپاه' specifies that these belong to the army.
segments.s295-g180: This relative clause ('طلایه که دارد ز دشمن نگاه') modifies or apposes the previous phrase. 'طلایه' is the vanguard, 'که' is the relative pronoun 'which/that', and 'دارد' is a present/factual verb 'has/keeps' (here 'keeps' a watch). 'ز' is the archaic preposition 'from/against', and 'دشمن نگاه' literally 'enemy‑gaze' = 'a watch against the enemy.' The clause describes the vanguard's role.
segments.s295-g181: This phrase repeats 'همان' ('the same' / 'that same') to mark repetition or symmetry. 'ساقه' (literally 'shaft' or 'staff') and 'جایگاه' ('place/station') are coordinated objects; 'بنه' here is a noun serving as the thing whose staff/station is being referenced. The line is elliptical: the verb (implied from previous lines, e.g. 'بیاراست') governs these nouns.
segments.s295-g182: Again beginning with 'همان' ('the same'), this short clause pairs 'میسره' and 'میمنه' with 'راست با' between them. Grammatically it is elliptical (the verb 'place/arrange' is omitted and carried over). 'راست با میمنه' means 'to the right with the right side' (i.e. set the named unit on the right beside the right flank). The exact technical sense of 'میسره' is specialized/archaic here and presented as a nominal unit paired with 'میمنه' (the right side).
segments.s295-g183: 'بیاراست' again is the transitive past 'he arrayed/arranged.' 'لشکرگهی' is a compound meaning 'army‑camp' (the place where the troops are encamped), and 'شاهوار' is an adjective meaning 'regal/lofty' modifying the camp. In classical Persian the modifier can follow the noun, as here.
segments.s295-g184: This locative phrase begins with 'به' ('to/into') marking direction/place: 'به قلب اندرون' = 'into the inner heart/center.' 'تیغ‌زن' is a compound noun 'sword‑striker' = 'swordsman.' Note the classical word order where the noun 'تیغ‌زن' is followed by the numeral phrase 'سی هزار' ('thirty thousand'), i.e. 'thirty thousand swordsmen.'
segments.s295-g17: A noun phrase introducing the person: جهانجوی is a compound epithet 'world‑seeking' (an epithet placed before the name). پر دانش literally 'full (پر) of knowledge (دانش)' is a descriptive modifier; in classical/poetic Persian the expected ezāfe may be omitted. The proper name افراسیاب follows as the head. The whole phrase means 'Afrasiab, who is world‑seeking and full of knowledge.'
segments.s295-g18: نشسته is a participial/past form meaning 'seated/settled' describing his state. به + place (به کندز) marks the location ('at/in Kunduz'). The repeated به before خورد and خواب is the poetic parallel construction meaning 'in/with respect to eating and to sleeping' — here خورد functions as a verbal noun 'eating' and خواب as 'sleep', so the phrase describes his occupations ('occupied with eating and sleeping').
segments.s295-g19: نشست here is the past verb 'he settled/took up residence'. اندر آن مرز = 'in that border/frontier' (اندر = in). زان کرده بود is a resumptive clause 'he had done that' or 'he had caused that' where زان ('from that/that') refers back and کرده بود is the past perfect 'had done'. The clause gives the reason for his settling.
segments.s295-g20: که introduces the content/explanatory clause 'that…'. Word order places the place (کندز) before the agent (فریدون) and the verbal participle برآورده plus auxiliary بود follows: برآورده بود = 'had brought about/established'. Grammatically this clause explains what 'he had done' — i.e. Fereydun had established Kunduz.
segments.s295-g21: برآورده is a past participle meaning 'built/erected/produced'. در کندز marks the location 'in Kunduz', and آتشکده is the noun 'fire‑temple' (object/thing established). The construction is poetic: 'a fire‑temple had been set up in Kunduz.'
segments.s295-g22: همه ('all/entirely') modifies the following predicates. زند here is used poetically as an intransitive verbal sense ('was blazing/was alive with fire' — a vivid, archaic use). استا is 'stood/was standing'. به زر آژده is a prepositional/adjectival phrase 'with gold, dazzling/terrifying' (به + زر = 'with/by gold'; آژده is an epithet meaning striking/dreadful/dazzling). Overall: 'it was all aflame and stood, resplendent with gold.'
segments.s295-g23: ورا is an adverb/pronominal locative 'there/for it' pointing to the place just described. نام کندز = 'the name (reputation) of Kunduz' (نام used as 'reputation'). بدی پهلوی = 'bad/evil among the Pahlavis' — پهلوی here names the Pahlavi/Parthian (or Iranian) side/group. The phrase says the name Kunduz carried a bad repute among that people.
segments.s295-g24: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پهلوانی is used as 'heroic deeds/tales' (here: 'heroism' or 'a hero's acts'), سخن 'speech/tale' — together 'a tale of heroism'. بشنوی is the present/subjunctive 2nd‑person of شنیدن used in the protasis of a conditional ('if you hear' / 'if one hears').
segments.s295-g41: زده is a past participle/perfective meaning 'struck' (here 'knocked') placed before the location phrase; بر در خیمه is a fixed prepositional phrase 'on/at the door of the tent' (در used in the sense 'door'). هر کسی is the indefinite subject 'whoever / everyone' and is positioned here to be completed by the following relative clause.
segments.s295-g42: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to هر کسی. نزدیک او is a spatial phrase 'near him'. آب بودش بسی is an existential/predicative clause: بودش (بود + enclitic ش referring back to the person) 'there was / he had', and بسی 'much / plenty' qualifies آب. Altogether: 'who had much water near him.'
segments.s295-g43: Noun phrases in sequence: برادر بد 'a bad/evil brother' (adjective after noun as common in Persian poetry), و 'and', چند جنگی پسر 'several warlike sons' where چند (several) modifies پسر and جنگی is an attributive adjective 'warlike/fighter'. The whole phrase lists people.
segments.s295-g44: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خویشان شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's relatives'. آنک is an archaic relative pronoun (equivalent to آن که / 'who/which') referring back to those relatives or their sons, and بد نامور is a predicative adjective phrase 'ill‑renowned / notorious' describing them.
segments.s295-g45: همی + past verb خواست forms a habitual/continuous past ('he was wishing / intended'). کآید is orthographic contraction of که آید ('that he may/should come'), with آید in the present/subjunctive expressing the intended action. به پشت سپاه = 'to the rear of the army' (به + پشت + سپاه).
segments.s295-g46: به نزدیک پیران = 'to/near the elders' (نزدیک used adverbially/prepositionally). ب + دان here function together as the fused demonstrative/locution 'بدان' = 'to that', attaching to رزمگاه 'battlefield' (i.e. 'to that battlefield'); the split in the text reflects poetic spacing. The phrase as a whole marks the destination 'to the elders' vicinity at that battlefield.'
segments.s295-g47: سحرگه is a temporal adverb 'at dawn' placed up front. سواری is the subject 'a rider/horseman', بیامد is the past 'came'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when', and گرد here functions adverbially 'around / about / rode about' (indicating movement/approach). The clause links his arrival to the following clause.
segments.s295-g48: سخنهای پیران is a genitive phrase 'the words/speeches of the elders' (سخنهای = 'words/sayings' + پیران = 'of the elders'). همه 'all' quantifies what follows. یاد کرد is a light-verb construction where یاد (remembrance) + کرد ('did/made') = 'recalled/remembered'.
segments.s295-g33: This prepositional phrase places the scene: به + پرده + درون + خیمه‌های پلنگ. به here functions as a directional/in locution preposition 'into/within'; پرده درون is a compound-like sequence meaning 'curtained interior' (پرده = curtain, درون = inside), and خیمه‌های پلنگ is a genitive/possessive group 'the tents of (the) Panther' (پلنگ used as an epithet). Word order is typical Persian poetical compression: modifiers follow the head.
segments.s295-g34: بر آیین introduces a circumstantial phrase 'according to/on the custom (ceremony) of…' (بر = 'on/upon', آیین = 'custom/ritual'). سالار ترکان پشنگ is a title + apposition: 'the chief (سالار) of the Turks (ترکان), Pashang (پشنگ)'. پشنگ is a proper name in apposition to سالار.
segments.s295-g35: نهاده is a past participle/verb used intransitively/passively 'placed/was placed'. به خیمه درون marks the location 'into/within the tent'. تخت زر is a noun phrase 'throne of gold' ('golden throne') where زر post-modifies تخت in typical classical Persian economy.
segments.s295-g36: همه پیکر تخت is a nominal phrase 'the whole form/body of the throne' (همه = whole/entire, پیکر = form/figure, تخت = throne). یکسر گهر follows as a predicate/adverbial comment meaning 'completely/utterly jewels' (یکسر = entirely; گهر = gems), so the clause says the throne's whole form was entirely of gems.
segments.s295-g37: نشسته بر او is a predicate 'seated upon it' (نشسته past participle used as verbal predicate; بر او = 'on/above it' — او refers back to the throne). شاه توران سپاه is a title cluster appositive: 'the king of Turan, (leader) of the army/host' (سپاه here apposed to شاه indicating his command).
segments.s295-g38: This is an instrumental/attendant-description phrase: به چنگ اندرون گرز = 'with a mace in (his) grasp/hand' (به + چنگ = 'in the hand/grip', اندرون intensifies 'inside/in hand', گرز = mace). و بر سر کلاه adds 'and a helmet on his head' (بر سر = on the head, کلاه = cap/helmet). Word order places instruments and accouterments after the subject as typical descriptive detail.
segments.s295-g39: ز بیرون دهلیز پرده‌سرای is a locative phrase introduced by the archaic poetic preposition ز (= از) 'from/outside': 'from outside, in the corridor/vestibule of the curtain-hall' (بیرون = outside, دهلیز = vestibule/corridor, پرده‌سرا = curtained-hall/stage-like hall).
segments.s295-g40: فراوان درفش بزرگان به پای states the scene: 'numerous were the standards/banners of the great (nobles) at the foot' — فراوان as adjective/adverb 'numerous/abundant', درفش = banner/standard, بزرگان = the great ones/nobles, and به پای functions spatially 'at the foot (of the tents/entrance)'.
segments.s295-g137: بر آن برنهادند یکسر is a verb phrase in past tense (3rd pl.) meaning “they undertook/decided upon that.” بر آن is a prepositional phrase (“upon that/with respect to that”) placed before the verb; یکسر is an adverb (“at once/entirely”) modifying برنهادند. که is the subordinating particle introducing the content clause whose subject follows (شاه).
segments.s295-g138: This is the content clause introduced by که: ز جیحون is a prepositional phrase (“from / beyond the Jeyhun”), بر آن‌سو specifies the target side (“on that side / over there”), and گذارد سپاه is the verbal nucleus: گذارد (3rd sg. past) “he placed / set” with سپاه as its object. Classical/poetic word order allows the verb to appear before or next to its object.
segments.s295-g139: که introduces a relative clause modifying قراخان. او is the pronoun subject of that clause and بود is the past copula (“was”); مهتر پسر is the predicate nominal meaning “the elder/eldest son” (مهتر functioning as ‘elder / principal’ before پسر). Word order places the copula بود between subject and predicate as typical in narrative past.
segments.s295-g140: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning “(he) commanded/said.” تا introduces a purpose/result clause here (“so that / in order that”) and is followed by رفت (past stem ‘went’ used idiomatically after تا to report the commanded action). پیش پدر means “in the presence of / before his father” (preposition-like پیش + noun).
segments.s295-g141: This clause predicates information about the father. پدر is the topic/subject and بود is the past copula. گفتی here is a parenthetical (literally “one might say” / “so to speak”); it is idiomatic and not the literal noun ‘conversation’ in this context. به مردی به جای is a verbless noun phrase meaning “a man of standing/rank” (به + noun indicating a quality; جای = rank/position).
segments.s295-g142: A list of attributes introduced by به: به بالا = “of high/lofty station,” followed by conjunctions (و) linking دیدار, فرهنگ and رای as further qualities. Each noun names a particular attribute (دیدار = meetings/visits/repute; فرهنگ = culture; رای = counsel/opinion). The serial use of به + noun phrases is a classical way to list characteristics.
segments.s295-g143: ز چندان سپه = “from/of that much/great a host” (ز = ‘from’). نیمه is the quantity “half”; او را is the clitic pronoun with the object marker را meaning “to him” or “him” as beneficiary/recipient; and سپرد is the past verb “entrusted/gave” (he entrusted/gave him half). Word order is flexible: the quantified noun phrase and object pronoun precede the verb in this poetic narrative.
segments.s295-g144: This short phrase describes the composition of those assigned: جهاندیده و نامداران = “world-wise (seasoned) and renowned” men. گرد here means “around/about” (indicating these men were about/attached to him, i.e., in his retinue). The adjectives/nouns are coordinated with و.
segments.s295-g201: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that/who'). باشد is the classical/subjunctive/potential third‑person form of بودن used here to express 'may be' or simply 'be' (common in epic poetry). نگهبان is the predicate noun 'guardian'; پشت پشنگ is a genitive/possessive noun phrase 'the rear of Pashang' — in poetic Persian the ezafe is often omitted, so پشت directly follows the name پشنگ to mean 'Pashang's rear.'
segments.s295-g202: نپیچد is ن + پیچد, a negative present/subjunctive (or optative) third‑person form meaning 'may not turn / does not turn' (classical poetic usage). سر is the direct object 'head.' ار is an archaic conditional 'if' (literary variant of اگر). بارد here is a poetic short form of ببارد 'falls/should fall' (a verb form, although the supplied senses list does not show this verbal sense). از ابر سنگ is a source phrase 'from (a) cloud of stones' (از marks source; ابر سنگ = 'stone-cloud' or 'cloud of stones').
segments.s295-g203: سپاهی here denotes a body of troops or force. به جنگ 'for/into the battle' shows purpose (به + noun). کهیلا is a proper name (the person who receives the force). سپرد is the simple past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' (3rd person singular: 'he entrusted').
segments.s295-g204: یکی 'one' (indefinite/number) is the subject; تیزتر is the comparative of تیز meaning 'faster' or 'more nimble.' بود is past 'was.' ایلای گرد appears as a name/epithet — in epic poetry proper names and epithets are often juxtaposed without an ezafe; گرد here is part of the name/epithet rather than carrying its ordinary lexical meanings, so it should be read as a name element.
segments.s295-g205: نبیره is a nominal head meaning 'scion' or 'offspring.' جهاندار is an epithet 'world‑holder' / 'sovereign' modifying the name that follows; فراسیاب is the proper name Afrasiab. The three words form a single nominal phrase (epic style often strings epithets and names without ezafe).
segments.s295-g206: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking this clause to the preceding noun. از پشت شیران literally 'from behind the lions' (از marks source; پشت = 'back/behind'). شیران is used poetically as 'lions' (often an image for brave fighters). ربودی is the past form of ربودن 'to seize/steal' (he seized/stole), and کباب is the object 'kebab(s)'. Word order is normal classical Persian: relative → source phrase → verb → object, with the subject understood from the prior noun phrase.
segments.s295-g207: جنگی here functions as 'warlike force/war‑band' (an adjectival noun). ز is the poetic variant of از meaning 'from.' توران is the land 'Turan'; سواران are the horsemen/riders. بدند is an archaic third‑person plural past form (here marking that such forces 'appeared' or 'were sent/formed'). The clause states existence/appearance of two Turanian war‑bands of horsemen.
segments.s295-g208: به دل 'into the heart'—به marks direction/target and دل is 'heart' (metaphorical: hitting the heart). یک به یک 'one by one' is a repeated numeric expression meaning 'one after another.' کوه ساران is a compound 'mountain‑people' (کوه = mountain; ساران used here in the sense of 'people/companions'), a poetic image for rugged fighters. بدند repeats the archaic past plural form indicating the action performed by those horsemen (they 'put/struck' into the heart); the precise lexical force is classical/epic and English idiom uses 'struck into the hearts' to capture the meaning.
segments.s295-g241: پراگنده functions adverbially here ('scattered/throughout') and precedes the prepositional phrase and verb for poetic emphasis. بر لشکر is the prepositional phrase 'against/upon the host' (preposition + noun). اسب is the (logical) object 'horses' placed before the verb افگنند; افگنند is a 3rd-person plural verbal form used in epic narration meaning 'they hurled/let loose' (classical past narrative style).
segments.s295-g242: دل و پشت ایرانیان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the hearts and backs of the Iranians' (دل = heart, پشت = rear/back) with ایرانیان marking the group affected. The verb بشکنند (3pl) follows and means 'they broke' (metaphorical: broke morale / routed); the subject remains the same 'they' from the previous clause. Word order (objects before verb) is typical of Persian poetry's flexibility.
segments.s295-g243: سوی باختر is a directional phrase 'toward the west' placed at the front for setting. بود is the past copula 'there was/lay'. پشت سپاه is the predicate 'the rear of the army' (پشت = rear/behind, سپاه = army) with the possessed/partitive relation implicit in poetic diction.
segments.s295-g244: شب آمد is a simple past statement 'night came.' The second clause uses به پیلان as an instrumental-like phrase 'by/with elephants' (به here marking means/instrument). ببستند is 3rd-person plural past 'they closed/blocked' and راه is its object 'the road/way'; poetic word order places the object after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s295-g245: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus said' with the verb گفت (here used as past speech verb) placed before its subject—a common poetic inversion. سالار گیتی فروز is a compounded title: سالار 'commander' followed by the epithet گیتی‌فروز 'world-illuminating' (two nouns/adjectival elements stacked as a poetic name).
segments.s295-g246: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. دارد is the present tense 'has/keeps' used here in the narrative sense. سپه چشم is a compact poetic compound meaning 'the army's eye' or 'the army's gaze' (سپه modifying چشم without an explicit ezafe). بر نیمروز marks the target/location 'upon Nimruz' (here the regional sense). The whole clause qualifies the commander: 'who keeps his army's gaze on Nimruz.'
segments.s295-g113: در گنج بگشاد is an older/formal past action: بگشاد (b + گشاد) is an archaic past-tense verb 'opened' (the b- prefix appears in classical Persian). در + گنج = 'in/into the treasury/treasury-house'. The conjunction و links this clause to روزی بداد, forming 'opened the treasury and gave out provisions.'
segments.s295-g114: دلش پر ز کین و سرش پر ز باد: دلش and سرش contain the enclitic possessive -ش (his). پر ز + noun = 'full of (something)'; ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' used in 'پر ز'. باد here is literal 'wind' used metaphorically ('head full of wind' = vain/fickle).
segments.s295-g115: گله هرچ بودش به دشت و به کوه: گله here is 'herd/flock' (not 'complaint'); هرچ ('whatever') + بودش ('there was' — بود + enclitic ش) = 'whatever herds there were'. The repeated به marks location 'in/at the plain and the mountain.'
segments.s295-g116: ببخشید بر لشکرش همگروه: بخشید (here with the b- prefix as past) = 'bestowed/gave out'. بر لشکرش = 'upon/to his army' (بر used in the sense 'onto/toward'), and همگروه is an appositive 'fellow-members/companions' — 'he gave them to his comrades in the army.'
segments.s295-g117: ز گردان شمشیرزن: ز = 'from'; گردان = 'troop/regiment'; شمشیرزن = 'swordsman(s)'. The phrase names the source group from which the number below is taken.
segments.s295-g118: گزین کرد شاه از در کارزار: گزین کرد is a compound 'choosed/did choose' (گزین + کرد), with کرد as the light verb marking past action. شاه is the subject. از در کارزار = 'for the field/battlefield' (از در = 'for/in the matter of' or 'to the field of'). Word order is S OV for the verbal clause (object/phrase before the verb complex is also common in classical Persian).
segments.s295-g119: سوی بلخ بامی فرستادشان: سوی = 'toward(s)'; بلخ = the city/region Balkh; فرستادشان = 'he sent them' (فرستاد + third-person plural object clitic -شان). بامی is ambiguous here (textually could be an epithet or a place-name); its dictionary senses ('on the roof') do not fit cleanly, so it is treated as uncertain/other in this context.
segments.s295-g120: بسی پند و اندرزها دادشان: بسی is an adverb meaning 'many/much' (here 'many/much advice'); پند و اندرزها = 'advice and counsels' (اندرزها is the plural); دادشان = 'he gave them' (داد + object clitic -شان). The clause is a simple past transitive construction: subject implied in previous clause, verb + object clitic here.
segments.s295-g209: سوی میمنه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the right/onto the right wing' placed before the object; لشکری is the direct object 'an army/troops' and برگزید is a past‑tense 3rd person singular verb 'he chose/selected'. Together the clause reads literally 'toward the right an army he selected' (standard Persian word order is flexible here, verb at end).
segments.s295-g210: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('and/so that/that'); خورسید is the subject 'the sun', گشت is the past of گشتن used as a copular/ resultive verb 'became/turned', and ناپدید is a predicate adjective 'vanished'. از جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from the world' specifying where it vanished from. Word order places the verb before the predicative adjective (classical/poetic flexibility).
segments.s295-g211: This is a noun phrase in apposition: a personal/title sequence. قراخان is the name/title, سالار a rank 'commander', چارم here functions as the ordinal 'fourth' (classical spelling), and پسر 'son' closes the phrase. In Persian titles and ordinals often precede the noun they qualify.
segments.s295-g212: کمر بست is an idiom 'he girded his loins/bound his waist' formed by the noun كمر + past of بستن; the subject is omitted (third person). و connects the next action, آمد is past 'he came', and به پیش پدر is the prepositional phrase 'into the presence of / before his father' (به + پیش + noun).
segments.s295-g213: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb (common in poetry); داد is past 'gave'. ترک چگل is a noun phrase 'the Turk Chaghl' acting as (part of) the direct object; the following numeral phrase ('سی هزار') in the line attaches to the following noun (see next segment). Word order: indirect object marker + verb + object phrase (poetic flexibility).
segments.s295-g214: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' (the principal object modified by the preceding numeral 'سی هزار' in the running text). و links to the following adjective phrase; شایسته کارزار is a bound adjectival phrase 'worthy/fit for battle' (شایسته takes کارزار as its object — here the ezāfe is unexpressed in the script). The full meaning: 'thirty thousand horsemen, fit for battle.'
segments.s295-g215: This is a coordinated noun phrase: three names/titles joined by و and capped by the head noun سوار 'horseman'. In such classical lines the final noun (سوار) applies to all preceding modifiers: 'the Tarazi and Ghazi and Khalakh horsemen.' The sequence is a stylistic listing; proper names or tribal labels are used attributively.
segments.s295-g216: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'the same' or 'that very', followed (in the full text) by the numeral phrase 'سی هزار' 'thirty thousand' and then آزموده 'experienced/tried' as a participial adjective before سوار 'rider'. The phrase therefore reads 'those same thirty thousand experienced horsemen' — آزموده modifies سوار and precedes it as a descriptive participle.
segments.s295-g193: "همان" is a demonstrative preceding the noun ('that/the same'); "نیزه آهنین" is a noun + adjective (noun first, adjective after: 'spear, iron'), and "داشتی" is the 2nd-person past form of داشتن ('you had'). Together they form a simple past clause: 'You had that iron spear.'
segments.s295-g194: This is a sequence of two actions: "آورد" (bring/carry) with the directional marker "به" and the locative "بر کوه" ('upon/on the mountain'), followed by the past verb "بگذاشتی" ('you placed/put'). The verbs are both past (narrative) and combine to mean 'brought (it) and put (it) on the mountain.'
segments.s295-g195: The verbless clause "پشنگست نامش" uses the fused copula (پشنگ + است) to state a name: 'Pashang is his name.' The following clause "پدر شیده خواند" has "پدر" as subject, "شیده" as the name applied, and "خواند" (here 'named/called') as the verb—i.e. 'his father called (him) Shideh.'
segments.s295-g196: "که" introduces the reason ('because/for'), "شیده" is the subject (the person named), and the idiom "به ... ماند" uses "ماند" ('remained/was') with "به خورشید تابنده" as a simile — 'was like/the same as the shining sun.' This is an older Persian way to say 'he resembled the shining sun.'
segments.s295-g197: "ز" is the poetic preposition 'from'; "گردان" is the group/troop and "گردنکشان" is a descriptive plural ('the proud/rebellious ones') modifying it—together 'from the troop of proud men.' The numeral phrase "صد هزار" follows as the quantity taken from that troop.
segments.s295-g198: Word order here places the indirect object/adverbial "بدو" ('to him') before the verb "داد" ('gave'); "شاه" is the subject 'the king'; "از در کارزار" is an adverbial phrase introduced by "از" ('from') with "در" meaning 'gate/entrance' here and "کارزار" 'battle/field' — 'the king gave him (them) at/from the gate of the battlefield.'
segments.s295-g199: "همان" ('that/same') modifies the following noun "میسره" (a gift/item here); "جهن را" is the recipient marked by the object marker "را" (Jahan receives the thing); "داد" is the past 'gave'; and "و گفت" ('and said') introduces the quoted wish that follows.
segments.s295-g200: "که" begins the reported wish/clause. "نیک اخترت باد" is an optative formula: "نیک" (auspicious/good) + "اخترت" ('your star', اختر + enclitic -ت) + "باد" used as 'may' (optative). The final phrase "هر جای جفت" ('every place' + 'pair/mate') functions as the domain where the wish holds—literally 'may your auspicious star be paired in every place,' an idiomatic blessing.
segments.s295-g81: This is a verbless nominal clause where the predicate-copula is supplied by the final منست (archaic). The two noun phrases 'قبا جوشن' (robe = cuirass) and 'اسب تخت' (horse = throne) are coordinated by و and read as parallel predications that the copula applies to. Word order is subject-predicate (noun + noun-as-apposition), with منست placed after the coordinated phrase to assert 'is / is fitting'.
segments.s295-g82: The structure parallels the previous part: 'کله خود و نیزه درخت منست' — two coordinated noun phrases ('کله خود' = 'my very head / my head itself' and 'نیزه درخت' = 'the spear [is] wood/tree') with the same copular predicate منست understood. خود is emphatic/possessive here ('own, very'). The poet uses درخت (literally 'tree') metaphorically for the wooden shaft of the spear.
segments.s295-g83: از این پس is an adverbial phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این + پس). نخواهم is the negative of the periphrastic/future-like want/will (here 'I will not / I do not wish to'); it precedes the coordinated verbs چمید و چرید, which form the predicate 'to chew/graze/browse' (archaic poetic verbs). The conjunction و links the two verbs as coordinated actions.
segments.s295-g84: و‌گر is the conditional 'and if / but if'. خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive 'themselves' or 'own selves'. تاج را uses the object marker را to mark 'the crown' as definite object. پرورید is an archaic/poetic verbal form from پروردن 'to nurture / cultivate'; here it functions as a subordinate verb in a conditional sense ('should nurture / tend'), i.e. an optative/subjunctive-like meaning ('if they themselves should tend the crown').
segments.s295-g85: مگر introduces an exception ('except for'). کین is a noun 'vengeance / grudge' and governs the following genitive phrase. آن نامداران خویش is 'those renowned ones of his' — آن is the demonstrative 'those/the', نامداران is a plural noun 'renowned ones', and خویش with the possessive clitic (ش) marks them as 'his own' (here 'his retainers/men'). Word order is demonstrative + noun + possessive.
segments.s295-g86: This is a coordinate apposition specifying who those 'renowned ones' are: جهانجوی (literally 'world-seeker', here 'ambitious ones') and خنجرگزاران (compound plural 'dagger-bearers', i.e. assassins/stabbers). The final خویش + clitic (ش) again marks possession: 'his world-seekers and his dagger-bearers.'
segments.s295-g87: بخواهم is a desiderative verb 'I would wish / I desire' (classical register). ز is the poetic preposition 'from / of' introducing the source ('of Kay Khosrow'). کیخسرو is the proper name. شوم‌زاد is a compound adjectival/epithet meaning 'born of / sprung (one's) birth' in the phrase — here the poet expresses a wish about parentage; the precise nuance is archaic/poetic ('be born as/through ...').
segments.s295-g88: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the wished-for outcome ('that ...'). تخم is 'seed / offspring' and سیاوش is the proper name 'Siavash'. به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in/to the world' (به + گیتی). مباد is an archaic negative optative meaning 'may it not be' or 'let it not happen' — here the speaker prays or wishes that Siavash's seed not exist in the world.
segments.s295-g185: نگه کرد is a compound past (نگه + کرد) meaning here 'kept/placed' or 'stationed'. The prepositional phrase بر قلبگه جای خویش follows the verb and specifies location: بر 'on/at', قلبگه 'the heart-place / central seat', جای 'place/position', خویش + ش the possessive ('his') — together: 'at his own central place'. Word order is verb → location phrase (typical Persian inversion for emphasis).
segments.s295-g186: This is a noun phrase in apposition to the place: two titles joined by و, سپهبد (a military rank) and لشکرآرای (army-commander/organizer). The final خوی + ش is the possessive suffix 'his', attaching to the phrase to indicate these officers belong to him (i.e. 'his spahbed Bad and his army-arranger').
segments.s295-g187: بفرمود is a courtly past used for 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces the subordinate clause 'that/so that' here functioning like English 'that'; پیش او شد پشنگ = 'Pashang came before him' with شد used as the past/actional verb 'came/appeared'. Word order places the subordinate clause after تا.
segments.s295-g188: که introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('for'/'because'), او is the pronoun 'he', and داشتی is the past tense 'had/possessed' (classical past form). چنگ و زور نهنگ is a coordinated object: چنگ (here 'claw' or grappling hand, not the musical sense) and زور نهنگ 'the strength/power of a whale' (a conventional epic simile).
segments.s295-g189: به لشکر is a locative phrase 'in/among the army' (به + لشکر). چون او is a simile 'like him', and نامداری is a noun 'renown/fame'. نبود is the past negative 'there was not'. The construction is existential: 'In the army there was not renown like his' → 'no one as famed as he.'
segments.s295-g190: به هر کار 'in every affair/undertaking', چون او سواری 'a horseman like him' (چون modifies سواری). Again نبود is the past negative existential 'there was not', giving 'there was no horseman like him in any task.'
segments.s295-g191: برانگیختی is a past verb meaning 'roused/raised/spurred' (classical/poetic past form). It governs two coordinated objects joined by و: اسب 'horse' and دم پلنگ 'the leopard's tail' (دم + noun = tail/edge/tip). The verb bears the action performed on both nouns.
segments.s295-g192: گرفتی is the past 'you/he seized/took'. بکندی is an archaic/perfective auxiliary or emphatic past ('did/wrench away'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and نیروی جنگ 'the force/power of war' (نیروی as 'strength' or specifically 'military force', جنگ 'war/battle'). Together they mean 'he seized and wrenched away from the power of battle.'
segments.s295-g97: کنون (adverb) opens the line meaning 'now'; گاه is the noun 'time/occasion' and کینست is the fused poetic form of کین + است, i.e. 'is vengeance' (predicate with copula fused). The infinitival آویختن is used nominally in parallel with کین (’hanging’), joined by و.
segments.s295-g98: ابا here is the noun/idea 'reluctance/refusal' used elliptically (classical Persian often omits an explicit negative or auxiliary), so the clause is to be read as meaning that there was no reluctance; گیو و گودرز are the agents (proper names) and خون + ریختن ('to pour/spill blood') is the verbal-nominal phrase 'to shed blood.'
segments.s295-g99: همم is the speaker's 'effort/concern' or 'lot' functioning as subject; رنج و مهرست is a predicate where مهرست is the fused poetic form of مهر + است ('is affection' or 'is devotion'); و هم introduces the second paired predicate 'درد و کین' ('pain and vengeance'). The parallel pairs are linked as qualities of همم.
segments.s295-g100: The preposition از ('from') appears twice to mark source/origin: 'from Iran and from the king (of) the Iranian land.' ایران‌زمین is a compound meaning 'the land/realm of Iran.' The coordinating و links the two از-phrases.
segments.s295-g101: بزرگان is the plural 'great ones/chiefs' and is followed by two genitive modifiers: ترکان ('of the Turks') and افراسیاب (specifying Afrasiab), creating a genitive chain meaning 'the chiefs of Afrasiab's Turks' or 'Afrasiab's Turkish chiefs.'
segments.s295-g102: ز (poetic from/upon) + گفتن ('the saying/hearing of the report') yields 'upon hearing.' بکردند is past 3pl 'they made/caused,' so the clause says they 'caused their mژگان (eyelashes) to be پر (full) of آب (water)' — a poetic way to say their lashes filled with tears. Note the causative/performative sense of بکردند here.
segments.s295-g103: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'that/because.' ما is the subject 'we.' سر به سر is an idiom meaning 'completely/entirely'; تو را is the object 'to you' (marking possession/relationship), and بنده‌ایم is the predicate 'we are servants.' The overall sense: 'that we are entirely your servants.' (مر here is an archaic/poetic element within the idiom and does not change the basic syntax: 'ما ... تو را بنده‌ایم'.)
segments.s295-g104: به expresses relation/means ('by' or 'before'); فرمان ('command') and رایت ('standard, banner') are the two things in front of which they feel سرافگنده‌ایم — a present-state adjective 'we are ashamed/abashed.' The verb is a present plural copular/adjectival form describing their humiliation.
segments.s295-g9: بخوردند is a past 3rd-person plural (خوردن) meaning 'they ate/consumed' and here heads the clause; یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/altogether' that scopes over the verb phrase; همه بار و برگ is the direct object noun phrase ('every/all the loads and leaves'), with the quantifier همه before the nouns. Word order is verb + adverbial + object (poetic inversion), so the verb appears before the object phrase.
segments.s295-g10: جهان را is the definite object (را marks the object) 'the world'; همی before the verb functions as a classical/perfective-continuous marker meaning 'kept/continually' (here giving an ongoing sense to آرزو کرد); آرزو کرد is the past action 'made a wish/wished' with کرد as the light verb, and مرگ at the end is the grammatical subject (common in poetry to place the subject after the verb).
segments.s295-g11: سپهدار ترکان is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the commander of the Turks' (noun + specifying noun). به بیکند بود is a locative clause: به + placename (بیکند) gives the location 'at/to Bikand', and بود is the past copula 'was' placed at the end as usual.
segments.s295-g12: بسی here indicates a great number/amount ('many' / 'much') modifying the following predicate; گرد او is 'around him' (گرد = around + pronoun او); خویش + ش is the combination 'خویش' (kinship/kinsmen) with the attached possessive suffix ش = 'his' (classical poetic spelling separates the parts); و پیوند 'and allies/bonds' completes the list, and بود is the past copula 'there were' linking the predicate to the subject. The phrase order emphasizes the plurality around him.
segments.s295-g13: همه نامداران is a quantified noun phrase 'all the renowned ones' (همه before the noun); ماچین و چین are two place-names or regions attached as modifiers ('of Maچin and China')—the ezāfe that would normally link them to نامداران is omitted in poetic register. The whole is a single noun phrase meaning 'all the famed men of Maچin and China.'
segments.s295-g14: نشسته is a participial/adjectival past form 'seated, sitting' used predicatively; به مرز کروشان زمین is a locative prepositional phrase 'at the border of Kroshan land' where به marks location, مرز = border, کروشان a place-name, and زمین = land/country. Poetic order places the participle before the locative phrase and omits a copula (implied 'are/sat').
segments.s295-g15: جهان پر ز ... is a construction 'the world full of ...' where پر ('full') follows the noun and is linked by the poetic preposition ز ('of/from') to the list of things filling it. خرگاه و پرده سرای form a coordinated list ('stables/lowly dwellings and curtained houses/inns'); پرده سرای is a compound 'parde-sarā' (curtained dwelling). The order (noun + adjective + ز + list) is typical poetic syntax.
segments.s295-g16: ز خیمه نبد is an existential negative construction: ز + خیمه ('of tents') followed by نبد ('was not / did not exist'), meaning 'there was no tent-place.' نیز 'also' adds emphasis, and بر دشت جای 'on the plain a place' gives the locative 'for a place on the plain' (بر = on/upon, دشت = plain, جای = place). Classical order places the negative verb before the adverb and locative.
segments.s295-g145: بفرمودتا is the fused classical expression بفرمود تا (verb + conjunction) meaning 'he commanded that...'. The following locative phrase در بخارا (preposition + place) gives the target location; بود here is a past copula used in indirect speech after بفرمود تا to report the ordered state ('be/should be'), so the whole clause functions as an indirect command ('he ordered that (someone) be in Bukhara').
segments.s295-g146: به پشت is a locative preposition phrase ('to/at the back of'); پشت here means 'rear/behind'. پدر is the noun being referenced ('the father' or a named forebear/place) and is immediately followed by the appositional place کوه خارا ('Mount Khara', کوه + adjective/name خارا). بود is the past copula 'was', so the segment states existence/location: 'was at the back of the father's Mount Khara'.
segments.s295-g147: دمادم is an adverb meaning 'constantly/again and again' modifying the verb. فرستد is the past form 'he sent' (classical past 3rd singular) and takes the compound objects سلیح و سپاه ('weapons and army/troops'), joined by و.
segments.s295-g148: خورش را uses the object marker را to mark خورش ('provisions/cooked food') as definite/ topical. شتر is the (agent/subject) 'camels'; نگسلاند is a past verbal form meaning 'loosened/detached' (here used in a transport context — classical narrations often use such verbs for loading/unloading or making camels bear loads) and ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from/along the road' indicating route. Together the clause describes camels bearing provisions along the road.
segments.s295-g149: سپه را marks سپه ('the army') with the object/topic marker را because it is the direct object of the following compound verb. ز بیکند places the origin 'from Bikand' (ز + place name). بیرون کشید is a light-verb combination: بیرون ('out') plus کشید (past of کشیدن 'to pull/draw'), so بیرون کشید means 'drew/pulled out (from)'.
segments.s295-g150: دامان تا is a phrase indicating extent along the 'دامان' (here 'foothill/skirts of the hills'); تا functions as an extent marker 'as far as/until'. لب رود جیحون is a genitive-like sequence meaning 'the bank/brink of the Jeyhun river' (لب = 'brink', رود جیحون = 'the Jeyhun river'). کشید is again the past of کشیدن ('drew/pulled'), so the clause says they were pulled along the foothills up to the river's bank.
segments.s295-g151: سپه بود is Subject + past copula 'the army was'. سرتاسر precedes the place noun رودبار to mean 'throughout/all along' the river valley: 'the army was throughout the river-valley.' Word order is typical classical Persian with the adverbial/scope word before the place noun and the copula after the subject.
segments.s295-g152: بیاورد is a past form meaning 'he brought' (bi‑ + آورد). It governs the following direct objects کشتی و زورق ('ships and small boats'), joined by و. The numeral 'هزار' that follows the objects in the verse (poetic inversion) quantifies them ('a thousand ships and skiffs').
segments.s295-g169: ز یک‌سو به دریای گیلان رهست is a locative phrase. ز (poetic از) + یک‌سو = 'on/at one side'; به دریای گیلان = 'toward/at the Sea of Gilan'; رهست is an archaic poetic copula/locative (literally 'راه‌ست' / 'lies/is a way') indicating existence/position. Together they set the location against which the following nouns are placed.
segments.s295-g170: چراگاه اسبان و جای نشست lists the things located there. چراگاه اسبان = 'pasture (for) the horses' (the genitive relation is implicit/poetic); و joins the next noun phrase; جای نشست = 'a place of sitting/settlement' (نشست here carries the sense of settling/encamping). The whole segment names what lay at that side.
segments.s295-g171: بدین روی is an idiomatic conjunctive phrase meaning 'for this reason/on this account' (بدین = 'by/with this', روی = 'face/ground' used idiomatically). جیحون و آب روان are two coordinated nouns ('Jeyhun and the running/flowing water') that form the scene or agents/locations tied to the following verb.
segments.s295-g172: خورش آورد مرد روشن‌روان contains the verb and its subject in the classical poetic order (verb before subject). خورش = 'food/dish' is the direct object, آورد = past 'brought', and مرد روشن‌روان = 'the bright‑souled man' is the subject following the verb.
segments.s295-g173: میان indicates a spatial 'between'. اندرون ریگ = 'the inner/inside sands' (an inward/inner part of the sandy area), و links the next item, دشت فراخ = 'the wide/spacious plain' (فراخ is an adjective meaning 'wide' modifying دشت). The phrase sets a spatial interval.
segments.s295-g174: سراپرده و خیمه بر سوی کاخ lists what stood there. سراپرده and خیمه are coordinated nouns ('inner-chambers and tents'); بر سوی کاخ = 'toward the palace' with بر + سوی marking direction toward the palace (کاخ). The phrase describes orientation of those dwellings.
segments.s295-g175: دلش تازه‌تر گشت زان آگهی is a causal-result clause. دلش = 'his heart' (دل + possessive ش). تازه‌تر = comparative 'more fresh/renewed'; گشت = past 'became' (change of state). زان آگهی = 'from that news/information' gives the cause (زان = 'from that', آگهی = 'information/news').
segments.s295-g176: بیامد به درگاه شاهنشهی is a simple motion clause: بیامد = past 'he came', به = 'to/toward', درگاه = 'court/presence', شاهنشهی = 'imperial' describing the court. The whole means he went into the royal court/presence.
segments.s295-g121: که is the relative/conjunction 'that/where' linking this clause to what precedes. گستهم and نوذر are personal names in apposition (poetic word order places them together). بد آنجا is an archaic/poetic locution ('then/there'); به پای = 'at the feet of' or more idiomatically 'among/in the presence of' (به + پای = locative phrase). The whole segment gives the location of those named.
segments.s295-g122: سواران روشن‌دل و رهنمای is a noun phrase: سواران 'horsemen/riders' modified by the epithets روشن‌دل 'bright‑hearted/clear‑hearted' and رهنمای 'guides/advisers'. The conjunction و simply links the two epithets/roles.
segments.s295-g123: گزین کرد is a compound verbal expression: گزین (choose/select) plus the past auxiliary کرد = 'he chose/selected'. دیگر 'another/further' modifies سپه 'host/army'. In Persian poetic word order the numeral phrase 'سی هزار' follows the noun to yield 'a host of thirty thousand.'
segments.s295-g124: سواران گرد indicates 'riders gathered/about' (گرد here = 'around/assembled'). از در is a poetic/prepositional phrase: از + در ('from/at the gate'); در in epic diction often means 'gate'. کارزار is the battlefield. The segment thus locates the riders as assembled by the battle‑gate.
segments.s295-g125: به جیحون فرستاد = 'he sent (them) to the Jeyhun' (جیحون = the Amu Darya). تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that/in order that', and بگذرند is the subjunctive/plural form 'they may pass/cross' (ب‑ prefix + present stem + ‑ند used here after تا to express purpose).
segments.s295-g126: به کشتی = 'onto/ by ships' (placing them aboard). رخ آب is a poetic phrase 'the face/surface of the water' (رخ = face, آب = water). را is the definite/object marker, and بسپرند is the subjunctive/intentional form of سپردن 'to entrust/commit' (they should entrust/commit), here meaning 'put them on ships to carry them over the water.' The object (رخ آب) is fronted for poetic effect.
segments.s295-g127: بدان is an archaic/poetic pointer 'for that/therefore' (a compact locution introducing purpose or consequence). تا introduces a temporal limit 'until', شب تیره = 'the dark night' (poetic), and بی + ساختن = 'without making/without preparing' (بی + infinitive conveys 'without doing X'). Together the segment sets the time and condition for the following prohibition.
segments.s295-g128: ز ایران = 'from Iran' (ز = poetic form of از). نیاید is a negative jussive/subjunctive 'should not come / may not come' used to express prohibition or intended prevention. یکی تاختن = 'anyone to charge/attack' (یکی = someone/anyone, تاختن = to charge/attack); the infinitive or verbal noun frames the action that should not occur. The whole segment states the intended result: no one from Iran should come to attack.
segments.s295-g89: This is a small clause in which خروشان (adjectival/adverbial) comes before the auxiliary بود with the continuous particle همی: همی بود = 'was (continually)'. The phrase ز این گفت و گوی is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of this talk' modifying خروشان همی بود — literally 'there was a roaring because of this conversation.'
segments.s295-g90: آگاهی آمد is a light construction where آمد (came) is the main verb and آگاهی (information/news) is the subject/object that 'came'. ز + proper name marks the source: ز کیخسرو = 'from Kay Khosrow'. بر اوی is a dative phrase 'to him' (بر = toward/to, اوی = him/her).
segments.s295-g91: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'. لشکر is the subject and رسید is the past tense verb 'reached/arrived'. به نزدیک جیحون = a prepositional phrase 'to/near the Jihun' (جیحون = Amu Darya).
segments.s295-g92: This is the result clause: همه روی کشور is a locative phrase 'over the whole face/surface of the country' (همه = whole/entire, روی = surface/face, کشور = country). سپه is the grammatical subject 'the host/army' and گسترید (classical past/pluperfect form) means 'spread/extended.' Word order is topical (locative phrase fronted) with verb at the end, typical of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s295-g93: بدان here is the fused adverbial phrase meaning 'with/at that' (ب + دان), marking circumstance: 'in that sorrow and lamentation'. سپه را is a definite object (را) 'the army/host', and بَخواند (here spelled بخواند) is the verb meaning 'he called/summoned' (an older/poetic infinitive/3rd‑person past form used by Ferdowsi to mean 'he called/assembled').
segments.s295-g94: ز پیران فراوان is an ablative/agentive phrase 'from the many elders' (ز = from/because of, پیران = elders, فراوان = plentiful/ many). سخنها is the plural object 'sayings/words', and براند is a classical verb form from راندن meaning 'he put forth/drave forth' — here idiomatically 'he uttered/sent forth (many sayings)'.
segments.s295-g95: This is a causal/motivating phrase: ز خون برادرش = 'for/because of his brother's blood' (ز = because/of, خون = blood, برادرش = his brother). فرشیدورد is the proper name and functions as the subject of the following action; the clause is compact and archaic, so the verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s295-g96: ز + instrumentals describes manner: ز رویین و لهاک = 'with iron (i.e., iron-clad / with iron armour) and with eagerness/haste'. شیر is the object 'the lion'. نبرد is the classical past/clausal form functioning like 'fought/engaged in battle' (in Ferdowsi's diction a noun/verb overlap is common: here it denotes the battle/act of fighting).
segments.s295-g233: A nominal apposition: the head noun سپهبد (spahbed) is followed by چو + name, where چو means “like/as.” The structure is noun + simile marker + name/place (گرسیوز پیلتن) with no verb — a common poetic appositional phrase equating or comparing the spahbed to the named figure.
segments.s295-g234: Another appositional phrase giving attributes of the spahbed: جهانجوی is a compound noun (جهان + جو) meaning “world‑seeker/ambitious one,” coordinated with سالار “commander/chief.” آن انجمن is a demonstrative + noun (“that assembly”) specifying which council; there is still no finite verb — these nouns further describe the spahbed.
segments.s295-g235: Simple past clause: بدو is the indirect object pronoun “to him,” placed before the verb (common in Persian for emphasis), داد is the past-tense verb “gave.” The direct objects follow (پیلان و سالارگاه: “elephants and the seat/house of command”). سالارگاه is a compound noun meaning the place or seat of leadership.
segments.s295-g236: A nominative apposition enumerating roles/titles conferred: سر نامداران (literally “head/chief of the famed ones”) and پشت سپاه (“the support/back of the army”). سر and پشت are used metaphorically (chief/support). There is no new verb here; these phrases further characterize what the gift or post amounts to.
segments.s295-g237: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase meaning “after that/thereafter.” گزید is the simple past “he chose,” and از یلان ده هزار is a partitive construction (“ten thousand out of the heroes”) with از marking the subgroup. Word order is adverbial phrase → verb → object (with the numeral following گزید in the source line).
segments.s295-g238: Relative clause introduced by که (“who/that”) describing those chosen. سیری (“satiety”) is the object, نداند is used as an archaic/historical present meaning “does not know” or “never experiences,” and کس here is negative indefinitive meaning “no one” (کس نداند → ‘no one knows’); از کارزار (“from battle”) gives the source/context. The clause therefore says they never know satiety from battle (i.e., are never sated/tiring of fighting). Note the poetic word order (object before verb, subject after verb) which is acceptable in classical Persian.
