segments.s295-g239: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning “he commanded/said (to do something).” It is followed by تا introducing a subordinate clause of purpose/command (“that/so that…”). در میان دو صف is a locative phrase “in the middle/between the two ranks,” giving where the commanded action should take place.
segments.s295-g240: به آوردگه is a destination phrase “to the place/venue (of meeting).” بر لب آورده کف is a participial phrase: آورده is the past participle “brought” used adjectivally and کف (“palm”) is its object, with بر لب (“to/on the lip”) marking position — literally “with the palm brought to (their) lips.” The implied subject (those brought) is omitted, a normal ellipsis in Persian.
segments.s295-g153: به یک هفته is a temporal prepositional phrase meaning 'for/after one week'. بر آب is another prepositional phrase 'on the water' (بر + noun). کشتی is the core noun 'ship/boat' and گذشت is a past intransitive verb 'passed/sailed' (گذشت here = 'passed by / went along'), so the clause reads literally 'for a week on the water a ship passed'.
segments.s295-g154: سپه بود is a simple past copular clause: سپه (the host/army) + بود (past 'was'). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/throughout' modifying the following phrase. همه کوه و دشت is a coordinated noun phrase 'all the mountains and plains' (همه = 'all/the whole of'), so the clause means 'the army was everywhere — the whole mountains and plains.'
segments.s295-g155: This is a compact, poetic prepositional phrase built with two instances of به: به خرطوم پیلان = 'to/at the trunks of the elephants' (خرطوم پیلان is a genitive/construct: 'elephants' trunks'), و شیران = 'and the lions', and the second به + دم gives a locative image 'at the tip/edge' (here functioning like 'at the stern' or 'at the extremity' in nautical/visual terms). The construction lists visual features decorating/moving on the ships.
segments.s295-g156: گذرهای جیحون is a genitive plural 'the crossings/passes of the Jeyhun' (گذرهای = passages of …). پر از is an idiom meaning 'full of' and takes the coordinated noun phrase باد و دم ('wind and breath') as its complement. The verb 'to be full' is implicit (the phrase reads 'the passes of the Jeyhun were full of wind and breath').
segments.s295-g157: ز کشتی is a prepositional phrase 'from the ships' (ز is the poetic form of از). همه آب is the noun phrase 'all the water' and شد ناپدید is a past-periphrastic expression 'became vanished' (شد + adjective ناپدید) meaning 'disappeared'. Grammatically this is a predicate formed with شد + adjective.
segments.s295-g158: بیابان آموی is a nominal phrase 'the plain of (the) Amu' (آموی is a genitive/relational form). لشکر کشید uses the past verb کشید (here meaning 'drew'/'pulled' or idiomatically 'drew in / took' / 'marched') with لشکر as its object/affected noun. Syntactically it reads roughly 'the Amu plain drew the army' (i.e. the plain absorbed or received the army, a poetic way to say the army moved onto the plain).
segments.s295-g159: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' (verb-first order is common in older/poetic Persian). پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards' and لشکر افراسیاب is the subject noun phrase 'the army of Afrasiab'. Together: 'Then Afrasiab's army came.'
segments.s295-g160: بر اندیشه رزم is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the thought/intention of battle' (بر + اندیشهِ رزم, where اندیشهِ رزم = 'the intention/thought of battle'). بگذاشت is a past verb meaning 'he put/placed' (here idiomatically 'he set' or 'he resolved'). آب at the end is an adverbial/complementive noun 'on the water' specifying the theatre ('he resolved upon battle on the water'). The understood subject is the previously mentioned Afrasiab.
segments.s295-g105: چو opens a comparative clause here meaning 'as' or 'like'. رویین and پیران are coordinated with و ('and'); both appear here as names (proper nouns) or definite referents rather than as common adjectives, so they are left in juxtaposition before the prepositional phrase ز مادر ('from/of mother'). نزاد is an archaic/poetic past/result verb meaning 'was born' or 'gave birth' (here passive sense 'was born'), so the clause reads literally 'as Ruyin and Piran were born of a mother.'
segments.s295-g106: The second چو repeats the comparative 'as'. فرشیدورد is a proper name; گرامی is an adjectival epithet 'honored/esteemed' modifying the following noun نژاد 'lineage/stock'. The word order is adjective before noun (typical in older Persian poetry), and the whole segment means 'as Farshidvard, honored in lineage.'
segments.s295-g107: ز here introduces the material or cause ('with/through' or 'from') and combines with the adjective خون‌گر ('blood-stained'/'bloody') that modifies the following coordinated nouns در و کوه و دریا ('gate and mountain and sea'). شود is the copular/auxiliary 'become' or 'are' used in the present/optative sense, so the clause says these places 'become/are made bloody.'
segments.s295-g108: درازای ما is a possessive construction 'the length/extent of us' (noun + pronoun). همچو is a comparative 'like/as', پهنا is 'breadth/width', and شود is again 'become' — so grammatically the subject 'our length' takes the verb 'becomes' and the predicate is 'like breadth' : 'our length becomes like (our) breadth.'
segments.s295-g109: یکی functions with the negated verb برنگردیم to mean 'not one' / 'not a single one' (a common poetic usage). برنگردیم is negative present/subjunctive 'we do not/shall not return.' ز این رزمگاه is the source phrase 'from this battlefield' and follows the verb, giving the full sense 'not one of us will return from this battlefield.'
segments.s295-g110: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. یار here means 'ally/companion'; باشد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of 'to be' (here 'be' or 'is'). خداوند ماه is a title-like phrase 'the Lord (or Sovereign) Moon' (خداوند 'lord/sovereign' + ماه 'moon'). The clause thus reads 'if the Lord Moon be an ally.' Note the inverted order (predicate after the verb) is common in older Persian poetry.
segments.s295-g111: دل شاه ترکان is a genitive construction meaning 'the heart of the king of the Turks' (noun1 noun2 order: 'heart' + 'king of the Turks'). از آن introduces the cause 'from that' or 'because of that', تازه گشت is a verbal phrase 'became fresh/renewed' — گشت is the past of 'become/turn'. So the clause means 'the Turk king's heart became renewed because of that.'
segments.s295-g112: از آن again gives cause 'from/by that'; کار here means 'deed/affair' and is the topic. بر دیگر اندازه گشت is 'it turned/changed (گشت) to another (دیگر) measure/degree (اندازه)'; بر here marks direction/toward ('to/into'). The whole segment says 'by that affair it changed into another degree/measure.'
